---
name: obsidian-markdown
description: Create and edit Obsidian Flavored Markdown with wikilinks, embeds, callouts, properties, and other Obsidian-specific syntax. Use when working with .md files in Obsidian, or when the user mentions wikilinks, callouts, frontmatter, tags, embeds, or Obsidian notes.
---

# Obsidian Flavored Markdown Skill

Create and edit valid Obsidian Flavored Markdown. Obsidian extends CommonMark and GFM with wikilinks, embeds, callouts, properties, comments, and other syntax. This skill covers only Obsidian-specific extensions -- standard Markdown (headings, bold, italic, lists, quotes, code blocks, tables) is assumed knowledge.

## Workflow: Creating an Obsidian Note

1. **Add frontmatter** with properties (title, tags, aliases) at the top of the file. See [PROPERTIES.md](references/PROPERTIES.md) for all property types.
2. **Write content** using standard Markdown for structure, plus Obsidian-specific syntax below.
3. **Link related notes** using wikilinks (`[[Note]]`) for internal vault connections, or standard Markdown links for external URLs.
4. **Embed content** from other notes, images, or PDFs using the `![[embed]]` syntax. See [EMBEDS.md](references/EMBEDS.md) for all embed types.
5. **Add callouts** for highlighted information using `> [!type]` syntax. See [CALLOUTS.md](references/CALLOUTS.md) for all callout types.
6. **Verify** the note renders correctly in Obsidian's reading view.

> When choosing between wikilinks and Markdown links: use `[[wikilinks]]` for notes within the vault (Obsidian tracks renames automatically) and `[text](url)` for external URLs only.

## Internal Links (Wikilinks)

```markdown
[[Note Name]]                          Link to note
[[Note Name|Display Text]]             Custom display text
[[Note Name#Heading]]                  Link to heading
[[Note Name#^block-id]]                Link to block
[[#Heading in same note]]              Same-note heading link
```

Define a block ID by appending `^block-id` to any paragraph:

```markdown
This paragraph can be linked to. ^my-block-id
```

For lists and quotes, place the block ID on a separate line after the block:

```markdown
> A quote block

^quote-id
```

## Embeds

Prefix any wikilink with `!` to embed its content inline:

```markdown
![[Note Name]]                         Embed full note
![[Note Name#Heading]]                 Embed section
![[image.png]]                         Embed image
![[image.png|300]]                     Embed image with width
![[document.pdf#page=3]]               Embed PDF page
```

See [EMBEDS.md](references/EMBEDS.md) for audio, video, search embeds, and external images.

## Callouts

```markdown
> [!note]
> Basic callout.

> [!warning] Custom Title
> Callout with a custom title.

> [!faq]- Collapsed by default
> Foldable callout (- collapsed, + expanded).
```

Common types: `note`, `tip`, `warning`, `info`, `example`, `quote`, `bug`, `danger`, `success`, `failure`, `question`, `abstract`, `todo`.

See [CALLOUTS.md](references/CALLOUTS.md) for the full list with aliases, nesting, and custom CSS callouts.

## Properties (Frontmatter)

```yaml
---
title: My Note
date: 2024-01-15
tags:
  - project
  - active
aliases:
  - Alternative Name
cssclasses:
  - custom-class
---
```

Default properties: `tags` (searchable labels), `aliases` (alternative note names for link suggestions), `cssclasses` (CSS classes for styling).

See [PROPERTIES.md](references/PROPERTIES.md) for all property types, tag syntax rules, and advanced usage.

## Tags

```markdown
#tag                    Inline tag
#nested/tag             Nested tag with hierarchy
```

Tags can contain letters, numbers (not first character), underscores, hyphens, and forward slashes. Tags can also be defined in frontmatter under the `tags` property.

## Comments

```markdown
This is visible %%but this is hidden%% text.

%%
This entire block is hidden in reading view.
%%
```

## Obsidian-Specific Formatting

```markdown
==Highlighted text==                   Highlight syntax
```

## Math (LaTeX)

```markdown
Inline: $e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$

Block:
$$
\frac{a}{b} = c
$$
```

## Diagrams (Mermaid)

````markdown
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start] --> B{Decision}
    B -->|Yes| C[Do this]
    B -->|No| D[Do that]
```
````

To link Mermaid nodes to Obsidian notes, add `class NodeName internal-link;`.

## Footnotes

```markdown
Text with a footnote[^1].

[^1]: Footnote content.

Inline footnote.^[This is inline.]
```

## Complete Example

````markdown
---
title: Project Alpha
date: 2024-01-15
tags:
  - project
  - active
status: in-progress
---

# Project Alpha

This project aims to [[improve workflow]] using modern techniques.

> [!important] Key Deadline
> The first milestone is due on ==January 30th==.

## Tasks

- [x] Initial planning
- [ ] Development phase
  - [ ] Backend implementation
  - [ ] Frontend design

## Notes

The algorithm uses $O(n \log n)$ sorting. See [[Algorithm Notes#Sorting]] for details.

![[Architecture Diagram.png|600]]

Reviewed in [[Meeting Notes 2024-01-10#Decisions]].
````

## References

- [Obsidian Flavored Markdown](https://help.obsidian.md/obsidian-flavored-markdown)
- [Internal links](https://help.obsidian.md/links)
- [Embed files](https://help.obsidian.md/embeds)
- [Callouts](https://help.obsidian.md/callouts)
- [Properties](https://help.obsidian.md/properties)

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /Users/liuyifu/Documents/123/MyLLMWiki/seeds/README.md
+# seeds
+
+这里放候选素材。
+
+它的职责是接住那些“值得留一下，但现在还不适合直接放进 `raw/`、`wiki/`、`outputs/` 或 `todo/`”的内容。
+
+常见情况：
+
+- 偶然看到的小工具、小方法、小技巧
+- 一段暂时还不算正式来源的摘录或线索
+- 带一点个人判断的记录，比如“为什么这个东西值得留”
+- 还没想好最终该归到哪一层的混合材料
+
+它不是这些东西：
+
+- 不是原始来源归档层：能明确作为正式来源保存的，优先进 `raw/`
+- 不是正式知识页：已经沉淀稳定的内容，应该进 `wiki/`
+- 不是任务结果层：一次问答、分析、报告，应该进 `outputs/`
+- 不是待办层：明确要做的事情，应该进 `todo/`
+
+目录约定：
+
+- `inbox/`：默认入口，新内容先放这里
+- `_templates/`：种子卡模板
+
+推荐做法：
+
+1. 先把新东西记成一张短卡，别急着第一次就分对类
+2. 卡片里保留“原始内容 + 为什么值得留 + 可能去向”
+3. 后面再按成熟度分流到 `raw/`、`wiki/`、`outputs/` 或 `todo/`
+
+推荐命名：
+
+- `YYYY-MM-DD-关键词.md`
+- 或者直接用能看懂的语义名，但尽量别过长
*** Add File: /Users/liuyifu/Documents/123/MyLLMWiki/seeds/inbox/README.md
+# inbox
+
+这里是 `seeds/` 的默认入口。
+
+新出现但暂时还没定型的内容，先放这里，不要求第一次就分清它到底是来源、知识、任务还是输出。
+
+如果一条内容后续已经很明确了，再把它迁走：
+
+- 正式来源进 `raw/`
+- 稳定知识进 `wiki/`
+- 任务产出进 `outputs/`
+- 明确待办进 `todo/`
*** Add File: /Users/liuyifu/Documents/123/MyLLMWiki/seeds/_templates/种子卡模板.md
+---
+date:
+type:
+status: seed
+source:
+tags:
+---
+
+# 标题
+
+## 看到的东西
+
+-
+
+## 为什么先放这里
+
+-
+
+## 后续可能去向
+
+- raw
+- wiki
+- outputs
+- todo
*** Update File: /Users/liuyifu/Documents/123/MyLLMWiki/首页.md
@@
 # 首页

# seeds

这里放候选素材。

它的职责是接住那些“值得留一下，但现在还不适合直接放进 `raw/`、`wiki/`、`outputs/` 或 `todo/`”的内容。

常见情况：

- 偶然看到的小工具、小方法、小技巧
- 一段暂时还不算正式来源的摘录或线索
- 带一点个人判断的记录，比如“为什么这个东西值得留”
- 还没想好最终该归到哪一层的混合材料

它不是这些东西：

- 不是原始来源归档层：能明确作为正式来源保存的，优先进 `raw/`
- 不是正式知识页：已经沉淀稳定的内容，应该进 `wiki/`
- 不是任务结果层：一次问答、分析、报告，应该进 `outputs/`
- 不是待办层：明确要做的事情，应该进 `todo/`

目录约定：

- `inbox/`：默认入口，新内容先放这里
- `_templates/`：种子卡模板

推荐做法：

1. 先把新东西记成一张短卡，别急着第一次就分对类
2. 卡片里保留“原始内容 + 为什么值得留 + 可能去向”
3. 后面再按成熟度分流到 `raw/`、`wiki/`、`outputs/` 或 `todo/`

推荐命名：

- `YYYY-MM-DD-关键词.md`
- 或者直接用能看懂的语义名，但尽量别过长

---

- `raw/`：原始来源层，只保留来源本身
- `seeds/`：候选素材层，先接住未分流的小工具、小方法、线索和混合材料
- `wiki/`：从来源编译出来的正式知识页
- `outputs/`：工作底稿、问答、报告等任务结果
- `_index/`：索引、协议、队列和运行记录

这轮重跑的默认原则是：

2. 链接尽量放在第一次真正需要它的地方
3. 对单来源迁移判断一律保守表达

# 总览

这页用来看当前知识库的结构和运行状态。

## 结构

# 维护协议

这份协议定义这座知识库怎么长，哪些内容放哪里，后续尽量按这个约定维护。

## 1. 分层

### `raw/`

原始资料层。文章、论文、仓库快照、数据集说明、图片都先放这里。这个层只读，尽量保留原貌，不做知识性改写。

仓库根目录下的 `Clippings/` 视为网页剪藏导入收件箱，不视为长期存储层。进入知识库前，应先把其中的文件规范化为 `raw/web/` 下的标准来源包，再删除 `Clippings/` 中对应原文件。

### `seeds/`

候选素材层。这里放那些值得留、但暂时还不适合直接归入 `raw/`、`wiki/`、`outputs/` 或 `todo/` 的东西。

它允许保留少量“原始内容之外的信息”，比如：

- 为什么这个东西值得记一下
- 当下的直觉判断
- 可能的后续去向

但它仍然不是正式知识层，也不是待办层。它更像分流前的稳定缓冲区。

### `wiki/`

编译层。把 `raw/` 里的资料整理成可阅读、可链接、可复用的知识页面。

- `wiki/sources/`：单个来源的摘要页
- `wiki/concepts/`：概念页
- `wiki/relations/`：关系页
- `wiki/topics/`：主题页
- `wiki/_templates/`：模板

### `outputs/`

派生产出层。问答、综述、slide、图表说明、阶段性分析都放这里。它们可以在确认后回流进 `wiki/`。

### `assets/`

跨页面复用的图片、附件、导出文件。

### `_index/`

索引、队列、规则。这里是整个库的控制面。

## 2. 命名规则

### 原始资料

`raw/` 里的内容默认视为已导入完成的原始来源，不再人工整理、不补总结、不改文件内容。

原始资料优先采用“来源包”形式，也就是一个来源一个目录，把正文、图片、附件、元数据放在一起。优先级高于“所有文件平铺在类型目录里”。

建议格式：

- 网页：`raw/web/<来源包>/<简短主文件名>.md`
- 论文：`raw/papers/<来源包>/paper.pdf`
- 仓库：`raw/repos/<来源包>/`
- 数据集：`raw/datasets/<来源包>/`
- 独立图片来源：`raw/images/<来源包>/`

来源包内建议结构：

- 正文或主文件：`<简短主文件名>.md` / `paper.pdf` / `README.md`
- 配套图片：`images/`
- 配套附件：`files/`
- 来源元数据：`metadata.json` 或 `metadata.md`

原则：

1. 网页正文和网页配图尽量放在同一个来源包里
2. 来自某个来源的图片，不单独抽到通用图片目录
3. 只有“本身就是独立来源”的图片，才单独放进 `raw/images/`
4. `raw/` 中如已存在旧结构，先兼容，不强制回迁
5. 网页或其他文本类来源，正文主文件默认使用简短直观的语义名；只有在容易撞名时才补最少的消歧尾巴

### 编译页

- 来源页：`来源名 - 标题.md`
- 概念页：`概念名.md`
- 关系页：`概念A - 关系类型 - 概念B.md` 或 `关系名.md`
- 主题页：`主题名.md`

### 输出页

建议格式：

- `YYYY-MM-DD 问答 - 主题.md`
- `YYYY-MM-DD 综述 - 主题.md`
- `YYYY-MM-DD 幻灯片 - 主题.md`
- `YYYY-MM-DD 图表说明 - 主题.md`

## 3. 页面职责

### 来源页

解决“这份资料到底讲了什么”。重点是摘要、关键点、证据、相关概念、相关主题。

### 概念页

解决“某个概念在整个库里是什么意思”。重点是定义、边界、争议点、相关来源。

概念页尽量承载单个原子概念。像“Spec 和 Skill 的协同”这类复合表达，优先写成关系说明或主题页里的组织语句。

### 关系页

解决“两个概念、来源或结构之间，到底是什么关系，以及这条关系为什么值得单独说明”。重点是关系定义、证据基础、适用边界、限制条件。

严格 KG 笔记法里，关系页是正式页面类型。但不是每条边都要建页，只有在这条关系本身值得持续补证据、反复复用、或者容易被误解时，才单独建关系页。

### 主题页

解决“围绕某个问题，当前库里已经知道什么”。重点是整合，不是重复摘抄。

### 输出页

解决“针对具体问题，这次产出了什么”。它可以是一次问答，也可以是一次报告、slide 或图表。它属于任务结果层，不等于已经沉淀进知识库本体。

## 4. 维护原则

1. 原始层和编译层分开，`raw/` 只读，不把总结写回 `raw/`
2. `seeds/` 用来接住暂未定型但值得保留的候选素材，不强求第一次落盘就分类正确
3. 每个重要结论尽量能追到来源
4. 优先增量更新，不全量重写
5. 有价值的问答结果要回写，不让思考只留在对话里
6. 新页面尽量挂到索引，不制造孤页
7. `outputs/` 可以先行承接阶段性结果，稳定后再提炼回 `wiki/`
8. 严格 KG 维护时，要始终有“关系页意识”；即使当前证据不足，也要区分“暂不建关系页”和“这不是关系页候选”这两件事
9. 一般任务仍按“先给方案、确认后执行”的协作方式处理
10. 对知识库维护任务，默认允许先执行后汇报；仅在高风险、破坏性、或存在重大歧义时再确认

### 知识库维护任务的默认范围

这里的“知识库维护任务”包括但不限于：

1. `seeds/` 候选素材的落盘、整理与分流
2. 来源包规范化
3. 远程图片本地化
4. `Clippings/` 收件箱到 `raw/web/` 标准来源包的迁移
5. `wiki/sources/`、`wiki/concepts/`、`wiki/relations/`、`wiki/topics/` 的增量生成与更新
6. `outputs/` 的落盘与必要回写
7. `_index/` 下的索引、待处理、日志、健康检查维护

### 知识库维护任务中需要再次确认的情况

只有遇到这些情况时，再停下来确认：

1. 需要删除、覆盖、合并已有知识页或批量重命名页面
2. 需要重构目录结构或迁移大量历史内容
3. 证据不足，但要输出强结论或大范围改写既有结论
4. 需要引入仓库外的新资料，且这会明显改变当前结论
5. 存在多个代价差异明显的路径，且当前无法合理默认其中一个

补充说明：

- `Clippings/` 中的原文件在成功迁入 `raw/web/<来源包>/` 后默认允许删除，不单独视为破坏性操作

## 5. 推荐工作流

1. 把新资料放进 `raw/`
2. 暂时不适合直接归入其他层的内容先放进 `seeds/inbox/`
3. 只有需要继续处理的项，再在 [[待处理]] 里登记
4. 为资料生成来源页
5. 从多份来源页里抽概念
6. 先补 frontmatter 邻接和 `## 关系` 里的轻量关系；证据够时再单独建关系页
7. 从概念、关系和来源页里组织主题页
8. 有新的分析结果时写进 `outputs/`
9. 确认有长期价值后，再合并回 `wiki/`

## 6. 当前默认模板

- [[来源摘要模板]]
- [[概念页模板]]
- [[关系页模板]]
- [[主题页模板]]
- [[问答输出模板]]
- [[Marp幻灯片模板|Marp 幻灯片模板]]

## 7. 运行面板

- [[待处理]]：当前收件箱和维护队列
- [[log|运行日志]]：记录每次较重要的 ingest、compile、query、回写、修补
- [[health-check|健康检查]]：记录结构问题、断链、孤页、待升级内容

# seeds

这里放候选素材。

它的职责是接住那些“值得留一下，但现在还不适合直接放进 `raw/`、`wiki/`、`outputs/` 或 `todo/`”的内容。

常见情况：

- 偶然看到的小工具、小方法、小技巧
- 一段暂时还不算正式来源的摘录或线索
- 带一点个人判断的记录，比如“为什么这个东西值得留”
- 还没想好最终该归到哪一层的混合材料

它不是这些东西：

- 不是原始来源归档层：能明确作为正式来源保存的，优先进 `raw/`
- 不是正式知识页：已经沉淀稳定的内容，应该进 `wiki/`
- 不是任务结果层：一次问答、分析、报告，应该进 `outputs/`
- 不是待办层：明确要做的事情，应该进 `todo/`

目录约定：

- `inbox/`：默认入口，新内容先放这里
- `_templates/`：种子卡模板

推荐做法：

1. 先把新东西记成一张短卡，别急着第一次就分对类
2. 卡片里保留“原始内容 + 为什么值得留 + 可能去向”
3. 后面再按成熟度分流到 `raw/`、`wiki/`、`outputs/` 或 `todo/`

推荐命名：

- `YYYY-MM-DD-关键词.md`
- 或者直接用能看懂的语义名，但尽量别过长

# 维护协议

这份协议定义这座知识库怎么长，哪些内容放哪里，后续尽量按这个约定维护。

## 1. 分层

### `raw/`

原始资料层。文章、论文、仓库快照、数据集说明、图片都先放这里。这个层只读，尽量保留原貌，不做知识性改写。

仓库根目录下的 `Clippings/` 视为网页剪藏导入收件箱，不视为长期存储层。进入知识库前，应先把其中的文件规范化为 `raw/web/` 下的标准来源包，再删除 `Clippings/` 中对应原文件。

### `seeds/`

候选素材层。这里放那些值得留、但暂时还不适合直接归入 `raw/`、`wiki/`、`outputs/` 或 `todo/` 的东西。

它允许保留少量“原始内容之外的信息”，比如：

- 为什么这个东西值得记一下
- 当下的直觉判断
- 可能的后续去向

但它仍然不是正式知识层，也不是待办层。它更像分流前的稳定缓冲区。

### `wiki/`

编译层。把 `raw/` 里的资料整理成可阅读、可链接、可复用的知识页面。

- `wiki/sources/`：单个来源的摘要页
- `wiki/concepts/`：概念页
- `wiki/relations/`：关系页
- `wiki/topics/`：主题页
- `wiki/_templates/`：模板

### `outputs/`

派生产出层。问答、综述、slide、图表说明、阶段性分析都放这里。它们可以在确认后回流进 `wiki/`。

### `assets/`

跨页面复用的图片、附件、导出文件。

### `_index/`

索引、队列、规则。这里是整个库的控制面。

## 2. 命名规则

### 原始资料

`raw/` 里的内容默认视为已导入完成的原始来源，不再人工整理、不补总结、不改文件内容。

原始资料优先采用“来源包”形式，也就是一个来源一个目录，把正文、图片、附件、元数据放在一起。优先级高于“所有文件平铺在类型目录里”。

建议格式：

- 网页：`raw/web/<来源包>/<简短主文件名>.md`
- 论文：`raw/papers/<来源包>/paper.pdf`
- 仓库：`raw/repos/<来源包>/`
- 数据集：`raw/datasets/<来源包>/`
- 独立图片来源：`raw/images/<来源包>/`

来源包内建议结构：

- 正文或主文件：`<简短主文件名>.md` / `paper.pdf` / `README.md`
- 配套图片：`images/`
- 配套附件：`files/`
- 来源元数据：`metadata.json` 或 `metadata.md`

原则：

1. 网页正文和网页配图尽量放在同一个来源包里
2. 来自某个来源的图片，不单独抽到通用图片目录
3. 只有“本身就是独立来源”的图片，才单独放进 `raw/images/`
4. `raw/` 中如已存在旧结构，先兼容，不强制回迁
5. 网页或其他文本类来源，正文主文件默认使用简短直观的语义名；只有在容易撞名时才补最少的消歧尾巴

### 编译页

- 来源页：`来源名 - 标题.md`
- 概念页：`概念名.md`
- 关系页：`概念A - 关系类型 - 概念B.md` 或 `关系名.md`
- 主题页：`主题名.md`

### 输出页

建议格式：

- `YYYY-MM-DD 问答 - 主题.md`
- `YYYY-MM-DD 综述 - 主题.md`
- `YYYY-MM-DD 幻灯片 - 主题.md`
- `YYYY-MM-DD 图表说明 - 主题.md`

## 3. 页面职责

### 来源页

解决“这份资料到底讲了什么”。重点是摘要、关键点、证据、相关概念、相关主题。

### 概念页

解决“某个概念在整个库里是什么意思”。重点是定义、边界、争议点、相关来源。

概念页尽量承载单个原子概念。像“Spec 和 Skill 的协同”这类复合表达，优先写成关系说明或主题页里的组织语句。

### 关系页

解决“两个概念、来源或结构之间，到底是什么关系，以及这条关系为什么值得单独说明”。重点是关系定义、证据基础、适用边界、限制条件。

严格 KG 笔记法里，关系页是正式页面类型。但不是每条边都要建页，只有在这条关系本身值得持续补证据、反复复用、或者容易被误解时，才单独建关系页。

### 主题页

解决“围绕某个问题，当前库里已经知道什么”。重点是整合，不是重复摘抄。

### 输出页

解决“针对具体问题，这次产出了什么”。它可以是一次问答，也可以是一次报告、slide 或图表。它属于任务结果层，不等于已经沉淀进知识库本体。

## 4. 维护原则

1. 原始层和编译层分开，`raw/` 只读，不把总结写回 `raw/`
2. `seeds/` 用来接住暂未定型但值得保留的候选素材，不强求第一次落盘就分类正确
3. 每个重要结论尽量能追到来源
4. 优先增量更新，不全量重写
5. 有价值的问答结果要回写，不让思考只留在对话里
6. 新页面尽量挂到索引，不制造孤页
7. `outputs/` 可以先行承接阶段性结果，稳定后再提炼回 `wiki/`
8. 严格 KG 维护时，要始终有“关系页意识”；即使当前证据不足，也要区分“暂不建关系页”和“这不是关系页候选”这两件事
9. 一般任务仍按“先给方案、确认后执行”的协作方式处理
10. 对知识库维护任务，默认允许先执行后汇报；仅在高风险、破坏性、或存在重大歧义时再确认

### 知识库维护任务的默认范围

这里的“知识库维护任务”包括但不限于：

1. `seeds/` 候选素材的落盘、整理与分流
2. 来源包规范化
3. 远程图片本地化
4. `Clippings/` 收件箱到 `raw/web/` 标准来源包的迁移
5. `wiki/sources/`、`wiki/concepts/`、`wiki/relations/`、`wiki/topics/` 的增量生成与更新
6. `outputs/` 的落盘与必要回写
7. `_index/` 下的索引、待处理、日志、健康检查维护

### 知识库维护任务中需要再次确认的情况

只有遇到这些情况时，再停下来确认：

1. 需要删除、覆盖、合并已有知识页或批量重命名页面
2. 需要重构目录结构或迁移大量历史内容
3. 证据不足，但要输出强结论或大范围改写既有结论
4. 需要引入仓库外的新资料，且这会明显改变当前结论
5. 存在多个代价差异明显的路径，且当前无法合理默认其中一个

补充说明：

- `Clippings/` 中的原文件在成功迁入 `raw/web/<来源包>/` 后默认允许删除，不单独视为破坏性操作

## 5. 推荐工作流

1. 把新资料放进 `raw/`
2. 暂时不适合直接归入其他层的内容先放进 `seeds/inbox/`
3. 只有需要继续处理的项，再在 [[待处理]] 里登记
4. 为资料生成来源页
5. 从多份来源页里抽概念
6. 先补 frontmatter 邻接和 `## 关系` 里的轻量关系；证据够时再单独建关系页
7. 从概念、关系和来源页里组织主题页
8. 有新的分析结果时写进 `outputs/`
9. 确认有长期价值后，再合并回 `wiki/`

## 6. 当前默认模板

- [[来源摘要模板]]
- [[概念页模板]]
- [[关系页模板]]
- [[主题页模板]]
- [[问答输出模板]]
- [[Marp幻灯片模板|Marp 幻灯片模板]]

## 7. 运行面板

- [[待处理]]：当前收件箱和维护队列
- [[log|运行日志]]：记录每次较重要的 ingest、compile、query、回写、修补
- [[health-check|健康检查]]：记录结构问题、断链、孤页、待升级内容

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
# 全局协作约定（系统级）

1. 我提出的所有问题，都不要直接改代码；必须先给出方案/改动点，并与我确认后再开始修改。
2. 对我提供的设计图：忽略其中的虚线边框（不作为需要实现的 UI/布局边界）。
3. 默认使用 `shuorenhua` 的表达规则回答：优先说人话、去模板感、自然一点，减少 AI 套话和空洞连接词；但不能改变事实、结论、数据、术语和代码语义。除非我明确要求保留正式/模板化表达，否则都按这个规则执行。
const additionalDeveloperPrompt = `# 必须遵守的行为规范\n
* 必须使用中文回答\n
* 在遇到需要探索源码的任务时，拆分出关键词，进行grep搜索，一次性读取所有相关文件进行分析，读文件时如果文件代码没有超过600行，不要分段读取，一次全部读入所有内容，必须在最少的交互会话次数内完成工作\n
* 编辑文件时，如果涉及到整个文件的过多次修改。不要使用Edit工具多次修改，而必须使用Write工具一次性生成文件。生成代码时，最大限度保持现有代码，不要随意格式化代码，只修改或增加必要的部分\n
* 模仿claude的风格回复用户\n
* 生成代码时，可读性一定要好，便于人类理解，但不需要很多注释。在保证逻辑清晰，正确的基础上要做到代码精炼，用最少的代码做最多的事情，但是不能破坏可读性。\n`
* 每次回复都喊我lelele
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.
- If workspace dependencies are missing, call `install_workspace_dependencies` first.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals or daily/weekly wall-clock schedules; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.
- If workspace dependencies are missing, call `install_workspace_dependencies` first.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals or daily/weekly wall-clock schedules; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.
- If workspace dependencies are missing, call `install_workspace_dependencies` first.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals or daily/weekly wall-clock schedules; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.aggregated_output:
---
name: myllmwiki-steward
description: Manage an Obsidian-based LLM knowledge base with raw/, wiki/, outputs/, assets/, and _index/ layers. Use when Codex needs to route a repository task by source type, normalize imported web clippings into standard source bundles, localize remote images, compile source/topic/method/concept/case pages from imported material, decompose long books, long reports, or other chapter-structured longform material through a working layer, synthesize multi-source topics, answer questions against the wiki, write reports or slides into outputs/, update indexes and logs, run health checks, promote stable outputs into wiki/, or review drafts for concept granularity, local organization, and narrative smell before finalizing pages.
---

# MyLLMWiki Steward

## Overview

Operate as the maintainer of a markdown-first Obsidian knowledge base built from imported source material, compiled into wiki pages, and continuously improved through outputs, Q&A, and maintenance passes.

Treat the repository as a layered system:

- `Clippings/` is a temporary web-clipping inbox that should usually be drained first
- `raw/` stores preserved source material and source bundles
- `wiki/` stores formal knowledge pages
- `outputs/` stores working layers, reports, and not-yet-promoted artifacts
- `assets/` stores shared reusable files
- `_index/` stores indexes, queues, logs, and maintenance rules

## Start Here

When this skill triggers, detect the repository root from the current working directory. Confirm that the root contains at least `raw/`, `wiki/`, `outputs/`, and `_index/`.

Read these files first before making decisions:

1. `_index/总览.md`
2. `_index/维护协议.md`
3. `raw/README.md`
4. `wiki/README.md`
5. `outputs/README.md`
6. the most relevant index pages under `_index/`

Then read only the templates and pages needed for the current task.

For repository-specific operating rules, read:

- `references/repo-playbook.md`
- `references/workflows.md`
- `references/page-contracts.md`
- `references/source-bundle-spec.md`
- `references/autonomy-policy.md`
- `references/relation-policy.md`
- `references/kb-governance.md`
- `references/longform-decomposition.md` when the task is a long book, long report, or other chapter-structured longform material
- `references/concept-granularity.md`, `references/organization-patterns.md`, `references/narrative-smell-tests.md`, and `references/good-vs-bad-examples.md` when you create or heavily rewrite topic / method / concept / case pages

## Obsidian Companion Skills Awareness

When the task overlaps with Obsidian-native formats or tools, explicitly consider these companion skills if they are available in the environment:

- `obsidian-markdown`: note structure, frontmatter hygiene, wikilinks, embeds, and callouts
- `obsidian-bases`: Base-style dashboards, schema views, and structured browsing surfaces
- `json-canvas`: `.canvas` generation or maintenance for visual maps and node layouts
- `obsidian-cli`: vault or app operations that should go through the Obsidian CLI instead of ad hoc shell behavior
- `defuddle`: cleanup of noisy web pages before or during source normalization

Default dispatch behavior:

1. note editing, templates, frontmatter cleanup, wikilink repair, embeds, or callouts -> `obsidian-markdown`
2. Base-style dashboards, structured browsing, schema views, or database-like outputs -> `obsidian-bases`
3. graph maps, relation maps, canvases, or visual vault layouts -> `json-canvas`
4. vault or app operations through commands -> `obsidian-cli`
5. noisy web-page cleanup before normalization -> `defuddle`

If the relevant companion skill is installed locally, read its `SKILL.md` before producing the artifact. If not, still produce an output shape that remains compatible with that skill family.

## Repository Task Routing Rule

Before acting, classify the task into one of these paths:

1. short-source intake or compile
2. longform decomposition
3. multi-source convergence around one topic or method
4. query / output generation
5. health-check or promotion work

Use `references/kb-governance.md` as the repository-wide control layer for all paths.

If the task is a long book, long report, or other chapter-structured longform source, also read `references/longform-decomposition.md` before creating formal wiki pages.

Use `references/page-contracts.md` whenever you create or rewrite page bodies.

Use `references/source-bundle-spec.md` for bundle naming, metadata, and media preservation details.

Use `references/workflows.md` for the exact step-by-step path after routing.

## Core Rules

- Keep `raw/` as the source-preservation layer. Do not write knowledge summaries or rewritten conclusions back into `raw/`.
- Prefer standard source bundles for imported material. One source should map to one bundle directory whenever reasonable.
- In each text-like source bundle, use a short semantic primary markdown file name such as `trellis.md`, `genericagent.md`, or `pl…2804 chars truncated…ross scenes, and where the boundary is
- treat `minimum actions` as a reusable action skeleton of about 3-5 moves, not as a full tutorial and not as a slogan
- each method page should have at least one concrete case; if the repository already contains multiple real uses, add a light `concrete uses` section or equivalent short case slices

Do not let one page type silently steal another page type's job.

## Draft Review Gate

Initial draft is not final draft.

If the task creates or heavily rewrites any topic / method / concept / case page, run a review gate before finalizing links, indexes, and logs.

Default review order:

1. run `references/concept-granularity.md` to decide whether concept candidates should remain standalone, be downgraded, or be merged back into host pages or `outputs/`
2. run `references/organization-patterns.md` to check whether the local chain actually unfolds instead of reading like parallel cards
3. run `references/narrative-smell-tests.md` to check whether the body can stand on its own and whether links are stealing explanatory work
4. if the result is still uncertain, compare the page against `references/good-vs-bad-examples.md`
5. only after that finalize links, indexes, queue state, and logs

Do not stop after a first draft when the page is structurally correct but still reads wrong.

## Longform Dispatch Rule

When the task is a long book, long report, or other chapter-structured longform source, do not jump straight from the whole material to final wiki pages.

Default behavior in this case:

1. define scope and current non-goals
2. read the skeleton before the details
3. build a chapter work layer under `outputs/`
4. collect candidate concept / method / case / topic pools
5. screen page types before formal page creation
6. create or update the source page, then continue the normal compile, indexing, and logging flow

Treat this as a specialized workflow inside `myllmwiki-steward`, not as a separate maintenance mode.

## Conservative Relation Rule

Start with conservative relations only.

Default safe relation families:

1. source -> concept: `定义` / `支持` / `体现` / `宣传` / `质疑` / `待验证`
2. concept -> concept: `相关` / `依赖` / `并列` / `容易混淆` / `属于上位主题`
3. source -> source: `互证` / `补充` / `口径不同` / `主说明` / `二手传播`

Do not infer strong causality, universal superiority, or proof-level claims unless the evidence is clearly strong enough.

## Confirmation Boundaries

Ask before proceeding only when one of these is true:

1. the change would delete, merge, or heavily overwrite existing knowledge pages
2. the change would rename many pages or restructure directories
3. the evidence is insufficient for the conclusion that would be written
4. new outside research is required and could materially change the synthesis
5. there are multiple plausible integration targets with non-obvious consequences

Deleting an original file from `Clippings/` after a verified successful migration does not require extra confirmation.

## Reading Strategy

Minimize round trips while staying targeted.

- use grep or equivalent search to collect related pages first
- if a relevant markdown file is shorter than about 600 lines, read it whole
- read the relevant templates before drafting new pages
- avoid bulk-reading the entire repository when the task is scoped

## Output Style

When answering the user:

- explain the maintenance judgment in plain language
- separate source facts from your synthesis
- call out missing evidence clearly
- suggest the next best repository action when useful

---

# Obsidian Flavored Markdown Skill

Create and edit valid Obsidian Flavored Markdown. Obsidian extends CommonMark and GFM with wikilinks, embeds, callouts, properties, comments, and other syntax. This skill covers only Obsidian-specific extensions -- standard Markdown (headings, bold, italic, lists, quotes, code blocks, tables) is assumed knowledge.

## Workflow: Creating an Obsidian Note

1. **Add frontmatter** with properties (title, tags, aliases) at the top of the file. See [PROPERTIES.md](references/PROPERTIES.md) for all property types.
2. **Write content** using standard Markdown for structure, plus Obsidian-specific syntax below.
3. **Link related notes** using wikilinks (`[[Note]]`) for internal vault connections, or standard Markdown links for external URLs.
4. **Embed content** from other notes, images, or PDFs using the `![[embed]]` syntax. See [EMBEDS.md](references/EMBEDS.md) for all embed types.
5. **Add callouts** for highlighted information using `> [!type]` syntax. See [CALLOUTS.md](references/CALLOUTS.md) for all callout types.
6. **Verify** the note renders correctly in Obsidian's reading view.

> When choosing between wikilinks and Markdown links: use `[[wikilinks]]` for notes within the vault (Obsidian tracks renames automatically) and `[text](url)` for external URLs only.

## Internal Links (Wikilinks)

```markdown
[[Note Name]]                          Link to note
[[Note Name|Display Text]]             Custom display text
[[Note Name#Heading]]                  Link to heading
[[Note Name#^block-id]]                Link to block
[[#Heading in same note]]              Same-note heading link
```

Define a block ID by appending `^block-id` to any paragraph:

```markdown
This paragraph can be linked to. ^my-block-id
```

For lists and quotes, place the block ID on a separate line after the block:

```markdown
> A quote block

^quote-id
```

## Embeds

Prefix any wikilink with `!` to embed its content inline:

```markdown
![[Note Name]]                         Embed full note
![[Note Name#Heading]]                 Embed section
![[image.png]]                         Embed image
![[image.png|300]]                     Embed image with width
![[document.pdf#page=3]]               Embed PDF page
```

See [EMBEDS.md](references/EMBEDS.md) for audio, video, search embeds, and external images.

## Callouts

```markdown
> [!note]
> Basic callout.

> [!warning] Custom Title
> Callout with a custom title.

> [!faq]- Collapsed by default
> Foldable callout (- collapsed, + expanded).
```

Common types: `note`, `tip`, `warning`, `info`, `example`, `quote`, `bug`, `danger`, `success`, `failure`, `question`, `abstract`, `todo`.

See [CALLOUTS.md](references/CALLOUTS.md) for the full list with aliases, nesting, and custom CSS callouts.

## Properties (Frontmatter)

```yaml
---
title: My Note
date: 2024-01-15
tags:
  - project
  - active
aliases:
  - Alternative Name
cssclasses:
  - custom-class
---
```

Default properties: `tags` (searchable labels), `aliases` (alternative note names for link suggestions), `cssclasses` (CSS classes for styling).

See [PROPERTIES.md](references/PROPERTIES.md) for all property types, tag syntax rules, and advanced usage.

## Tags

```markdown
#tag                    Inline tag
#nested/tag             Nested tag with hierarchy
```

Tags can contain letters, numbers (not first character), underscores, hyphens, and forward slashes. Tags can also be defined in frontmatter under the `tags` property.

## Comments

```markdown
This is visible %%but this is hidden%% text.

%%
This entire block is hidden in reading view.
%%
```

## Obsidian-Specific Formatting

```markdown
==Highlighted text==                   Highlight syntax
```

## Math (LaTeX)

```markdown
Inline: $e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$

Block:
$$
\frac{a}{b} = c
$$
```

## Diagrams (Mermaid)

````markdown
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start] --> B{Decision}
    B -->|Yes| C[Do this]
    B -->|No| D[Do that]
```
````

To link Mermaid nodes to Obsidian notes, add `class NodeName internal-link;`.

## Footnotes

```markdown
Text with a footnote[^1].

[^1]: Footnote content.

Inline footnote.^[This is inline.]
```

## Complete Example

````markdown
---
title: Project Alpha
date: 2024-01-15
tags:
  - project
  - active
status: in-progress
---

# Project Alpha

This project aims to [[improve workflow]] using modern techniques.

> [!important] Key Deadline
> The first milestone is due on ==January 30th==.

## Tasks

- [x] Initial planning
- [ ] Development phase
  - [ ] Backend implementation
  - [ ] Frontend design

## Notes

The algorithm uses $O(n \log n)$ sorting. See [[Algorithm Notes#Sorting]] for details.

![[Architecture Diagram.png|600]]

Reviewed in [[Meeting Notes 2024-01-10#Decisions]].
````

## References

- [Obsidian Flavored Markdown](https://help.obsidian.md/obsidian-flavored-markdown)
- [Internal links](https://help.obsidian.md/links)
- [Embed files](https://help.obsidian.md/embeds)
- [Callouts](https://help.obsidian.md/callouts)
- [Properties](https://help.obsidian.md/properties)

name: myllmwiki-steward
description: Manage an Obsidian-based LLM knowledge base with raw/, wiki/, outputs/, assets/, and _index/ layers. Use when Codex needs to route a repository task by source type, normalize imported web clippings into standard source bundles, localize remote images, compile source/topic/method/concept/case pages from imported material, decompose long books, long reports, or other chapter-structured longform material through a working layer, synthesize multi-source topics, answer questions against the wiki, write reports or slides into outputs/, update indexes and logs, run health checks, promote stable outputs into wiki/, or review drafts for concept granularity, local organization, and narrative smell before finalizing pages.
---

# MyLLMWiki Steward

## Overview

Operate as the maintainer of a markdown-first Obsidian knowledge base built from imported source material, compiled into wiki pages, and continuously improved through outputs, Q&A, and maintenance passes.

Treat the repository as a layered system:

- `Clippings/` is a temporary web-clipping inbox that should usually be drained first
- `raw/` stores preserved source material and source bundles
- `wiki/` stores formal knowledge pages
- `outputs/` stores working layers, reports, and not-yet-promoted artifacts
- `assets/` stores shared reusable files
- `_index/` stores indexes, queues, logs, and maintenance rules

## Start Here

When this skill triggers, detect the repository root from the current working directory. Confirm that the root contains at least `raw/`, `wiki/`, `outputs/`, and `_index/`.

Read these files first before making decisions:

1. `_index/总览.md`
2. `_index/维护协议.md`
3. `raw/README.md`
4. `wiki/README.md`
5. `outputs/README.md`
6. the most relevant index pages under `_index/`

Then read only the templates and pages needed for the current task.

For repository-specific operating rules, read:

- `references/repo-playbook.md`
- `references/workflows.md`
- `references/page-contracts.md`
- `references/source-bundle-spec.md`
- `references/autonomy-policy.md`
- `references/relation-policy.md`
- `references/kb-governance.md`
- `references/longform-decomposition.md` when the task is a long book, long report, or other chapter-structured longform material
- `references/concept-granularity.md`, `references/organization-patterns.md`, `references/narrative-smell-tests.md`, and `references/good-vs-bad-examples.md` when you create or heavily rewrite topic / method / concept / case pages

## Obsidian Companion Skills Awareness

When the task overlaps with Obsidian-native formats or tools, explicitly consider these companion skills if they are available in the environment:

- `obsidian-markdown`: note structure, frontmatter hygiene, wikilinks, embeds, and callouts
- `obsidian-bases`: Base-style dashboards, schema views, and structured browsing surfaces
- `json-canvas`: `.canvas` generation or maintenance for visual maps and node layouts
- `obsidian-cli`: vault or app operations that should go through the Obsidian CLI instead of ad hoc shell behavior
- `defuddle`: cleanup of noisy web pages before or during source normalization

Default dispatch behavior:

1. note editing, templates, frontmatter cleanup, wikilink repair, embeds, or callouts -> `obsidian-markdown`
2. Base-style dashboards, structured browsing, schema views, or database-like outputs -> `obsidian-bases`
3. graph maps, relation maps, canvases, or visual vault layouts -> `json-canvas`
4. vault or app operations through commands -> `obsidian-cli`
5. noisy web-page cleanup before normalization -> `defuddle`

If the relevant companion skill is installed locally, read its `SKILL.md` before producing the artifact. If not, still produce an output shape that remains compatible with that skill family.

## Repository Task Routing Rule

Before acting, classify the task into one of these paths:

1. short-source intake or compile
2. longform decomposition
3. multi-source convergence around one topic or method
4. query / output generation
5. health-check or promotion work

Use `references/kb-governance.md` as the repository-wide control layer for all paths.

If the task is a long book, long report, or other chapter-structured longform source, also read `references/longform-decomposition.md` before creating formal wiki pages.

Use `references/page-contracts.md` whenever you create or rewrite page bodies.

Use `references/source-bundle-spec.md` for bundle naming, metadata, and media preservation details.

Use `references/workflows.md` for the exact step-by-step path after routing.

## Core Rules

- Keep `raw/` as the source-preservation layer. Do not write knowledge summaries or rewritten conclusions back into `raw/`.
- Prefer standard source bundles for imported material. One source should map to one bundle directory whenever reasonable.
- In each text-like source bundle, use a short semantic primary markdown file name such as `trellis.md`, `genericagent.md`, or `plus-team-channels.md`. Add only the smallest useful suffix when disambiguation is needed.
- Treat `outputs/` as a legitimate working layer, not as a trash bin. Long materials should usually pass through `outputs/` before formal wiki pages are created.
- A formal maintenance pass should usually create or update a source page before downstream topic / method / concept / case pages are expanded.
- Keep every important conclusion traceable to one or more source pages.
- Write source-facing statements before maintainer synthesis. Prefer document-grounded phrasing over free extrapolation.
- Keep transfer claims conservative. Prefer formulations such as `当前来源支持`, `当前可保守迁出`, `可以低强度复用`, and `更强结论仍待验证` when the stronger claim is not directly supported.
- Prefer explicit links over implied relationships. Reduce orphan pages.
- For links between knowledge pages inside `wiki/` and `_index/`, prefer Obsidian `[[wikilink]]` over path-style markdown links.
- Keep normal markdown links for raw evidence paths, downloadable files, and external URLs.
- Update indexes and logs when a change materially affects discoverability or maintenance state.
- For normal maintenance tasks, default to end-to-end execution and report after the work is done.
- Pause for confirmation only when the action is high-risk, destructive, or materially ambiguous.

## Page-Role Rule

Keep these page roles distinct:

- source page: entry point and evidence sink for one source
- topic page: local organization for one question or problem space
- method page: invocation timing for a reusable judgment or action sequence
- concept page: minimum shared definition for a reusable idea
- case page: concrete landing point with object, scene, and process
- domain page: broader navigation layer when long-term curation warrants it
- relation page: standalone relation explanation only when the relation itself has durable reuse value

Topic-page writing rule:

- topic pages should explain how the thing naturally advances, not foreground methods as named modules
- when a method is relevant, write the move itself first, then let the method link hang off that move as a supplementary exit
- avoid wording like `这里调用某方法` and avoid footer-style `相关方法` blocks carrying more structure than the body
- in short: topic page writes the action; method page writes why that action deserves standalone reuse

Method-page writing rule:

- method pages should not stop at `why use this` and `when to invoke it`; they must also explain what this move is actually doing and the minimum way to carry it out
- default method-page questions are: what stuck point it repairs, what wrong reaction it corrects, what this step is actually doing, the minimum actions, when to invoke it, one minimal case, optional in-repo concrete uses, how it deforms across scenes, and where the boundary is
- treat `minimum actions` as a reusable action skeleton of about 3-5 moves, not as a full tutorial and not as a slogan
- each method page should have at least one concrete case; if the repository already contains multiple real uses, add a light `concrete uses` section or equivalent short case slices

Do not let one page type silently steal another page type's job.

## Draft Review Gate

Initial draft is not final draft.

If the task creates or heavily rewrites any topic / method / concept / case page, run a review gate before finalizing links, indexes, and logs.

Default review order:

1. run `references/concept-granularity.md` to decide whether concept candidates should remain standalone, be downgraded, or be merged back into host pages or `outputs/`
2. run `references/organization-patterns.md` to check whether the local chain actually unfolds instead of reading like parallel cards
3. run `references/narrative-smell-tests.md` to check whether the body can stand on its own and whether links are stealing explanatory work
4. if the result is still uncertain, compare the page against `references/good-vs-bad-examples.md`
5. only after that finalize links, indexes, queue state, and logs

Do not stop after a first draft when the page is structurally correct but still reads wrong.

## Longform Dispatch Rule

When the task is a long book, long report, or other chapter-structured longform source, do not jump straight from the whole material to final wiki pages.

Default behavior in this case:

1. define scope and current non-goals
2. read the skeleton before the details
3. build a chapter work layer under `outputs/`
4. collect candidate concept / method / case / topic pools
5. screen page types before formal page creation
6. create or update the source page, then continue the normal compile, indexing, and logging flow

Treat this as a specialized workflow inside `myllmwiki-steward`, not as a separate maintenance mode.

## Conservative Relation Rule

Start with conservative relations only.

Default safe relation families:

1. source -> concept: `定义` / `支持` / `体现` / `宣传` / `质疑` / `待验证`
2. concept -> concept: `相关` / `依赖` / `并列` / `容易混淆` / `属于上位主题`
3. source -> source: `互证` / `补充` / `口径不同` / `主说明` / `二手传播`

Do not infer strong causality, universal superiority, or proof-level claims unless the evidence is clearly strong enough.

## Confirmation Boundaries

Ask before proceeding only when one of these is true:

1. the change would delete, merge, or heavily overwrite existing knowledge pages
2. the change would rename many pages or restructure directories
3. the evidence is insufficient for the conclusion that would be written
4. new outside research is required and could materially change the synthesis
5. there are multiple plausible integration targets with non-obvious consequences

Deleting an original file from `Clippings/` after a verified successful migration does not require extra confirmation.

## Reading Strategy

Minimize round trips while staying targeted.

- use grep or equivalent search to collect related pages first
- if a relevant markdown file is shorter than about 600 lines, read it whole
- read the relevant templates before drafting new pages
- avoid bulk-reading the entire repository when the task is scoped

## Output Style

When answering the user:

- explain the maintenance judgment in plain language
- separate source facts from your synthesis
- call out missing evidence clearly
- suggest the next best repository action when useful

## References

- `references/repo-playbook.md`: repository map, index surfaces, templates, and maintenance boundaries
- `references/workflows.md`: task routing and step-by-step operating paths
- `references/page-contracts.md`: role boundaries, minimum structure, and quality checks for page types
- `references/source-bundle-spec.md`: bundle layout, naming, metadata, and image-localization rules
- `references/autonomy-policy.md`: default execution scope and confirmation boundaries
